skill.name: lark-skill-maker
skill.description: Create reusable lark-cli skills that wrap Lark OpenAPI calls and multi-step Lark workflows.
skill.summary: Guides agents through creating custom lark-cli skills for Lark API automation.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: The static command findings are mostly markdown examples for lark-cli, not hidden executable code. The skill intentionally teaches agents to use lark-cli and raw Lark OpenAPI calls, including POST requests, so it carries legitimate external command and remote data mutation risk that should be disclosed. The path traversal, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and entropy alerts appear to be false positives from markdown links, frontmatter, Chinese prose, and template text.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate External Command and API Mutation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents lark-cli commands and raw Lark OpenAPI calls, including POST examples. This is the intended function, but an agent following the skill could read or modify Lark tenant data, so users should confirm scopes and write actions before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible in markdown and match the stated purpose of building Lark CLI skills. Risk depends on how an agent executes generated commands and whether users approve write operations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged inline backticks and fenced command examples. These are documentation examples, not embedded scripts or automatic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited command pattern is inside markdown prose or a fenced example. No script file, hook, or executable payload was found in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The relative paths point to neighboring skill documentation files. They do not instruct file reads outside the repository or construct paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal tokens appear only in markdown links to documented sibling skills. There is no code path that dereferences user-controlled filesystem input.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography lines contain frontmatter, description text, and template wording. No cryptographic function, hashing API, encoded payload, or encrypted blob was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are readable markdown and YAML metadata. The high entropy signal is likely caused by non-English prose and does not correlate with obfuscated code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is Template Prose
low_findings.description: The flagged line is a template comment about using an ID from a previous step. It does not collect host, network, user, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a markdown template for multi-step API workflows. No reconnaissance command or host inspection instruction is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Lark OpenAPI Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches use of lark-cli api with arbitrary OpenAPI paths and request bodies. This pattern is powerful and legitimate, but it can affect Lark data if used with write methods.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The raw API command pattern is explicit and semantically relevant to the skill. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the same file recommends confirmation and dry-run behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often repeat the same Lark API research before automating office workflows. This skill provides a compact process for turning lark-cli commands into reusable skills with permissions and safety notes.
actual_capabilities: Explains the lark-cli command hierarchy for shortcuts, registered APIs, and raw OpenAPI calls.
Guides API discovery with lark-cli help, schema lookup, and direct OpenAPI requests.
Provides a SKILL.md template for new Lark automation skills.
Prompts authors to document required Lark permission scopes.
Encourages user confirmation and dry-run previews before write operations.
limitations: Requires lark-cli to be installed and available to the agent.
Requires valid Lark authentication and tenant permissions.
Does not validate every generated API path or scope automatically.
Generated skills still need human review before production use.

use_cases.title: Package Repeated Lark Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn a repeated Lark operation into a skill with clear commands, inputs, and permission notes.

use_cases.title: Document Internal API Workflows
use_cases.description: Create a reusable workflow guide for multi-step Lark API actions that pass IDs between steps.

use_cases.title: Standardize Lark Automation Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the template to require scopes, dry-run behavior, and write confirmation in new skills.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for one Lark API action.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a lark-cli skill for this Lark task: [task]. Include the command, required inputs, and permission scope.

prompt_templates.title: Research an API First
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for finding the right Lark API before writing the skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research the lark-cli command or OpenAPI endpoint for [workflow]. Then draft a skill with parameters and safety notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for workflows that pass data between API calls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a lark-cli skill for [workflow]. Show each step, the data passed between steps, permissions, and rollback considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Draft Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for hardening a generated skill before use.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this draft Lark skill for missing scopes, unsafe write operations, unclear inputs, and incomplete dry-run guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for searching Lark meeting rooms by floor.
output_examples.output: A skill outline with a clear trigger description, the relevant meeting room lookup flow, expected inputs, and required permission notes.

output_examples.input: Draft a skill for creating a Lark resource and then updating related metadata.
output: A multi-step workflow that records IDs between steps.
A permission table for each operation.
A safety section that asks for confirmation before write actions.

output_examples.input: Review a new Lark automation skill before publishing.
output_examples.output: A review summary covering command clarity, scope coverage, write safeguards, failure handling, and missing prerequisites.

best_practices: Confirm user intent before any command that creates, updates, or deletes Lark data.
Document exact permission scopes for every API action in the generated skill.
Prefer registered lark-cli commands or shortcuts before raw OpenAPI calls.
anti_patterns: Do not publish a generated skill without checking its required scopes.
Do not hide write operations inside broad workflow descriptions.
Do not use raw OpenAPI paths when a supported lark-cli command is available.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create SKILL.md files that teach agents how to use lark-cli for Lark API workflows.

faq.question: Does it require lark-cli?
faq.answer: Yes. The generated workflows assume lark-cli is installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it call any Lark OpenAPI endpoint?
faq.answer: It explains how to use raw OpenAPI calls when shortcuts or registered APIs do not cover the task.

faq.question: Is this safe for write operations?
faq.answer: It includes guidance to confirm user intent and preview changes, but reviewers should still check every write command.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for developers and operations teams that maintain repeatable Lark automation workflows.

faq.question: Does it validate Lark permission scopes?
faq.answer: No. It reminds authors to document scopes, but final scope validation requires Lark documentation or tenant testing.
